channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: John Lithgow’s Career Spans 200 Roles — From ‘3rd Rock’ to Roald Dahl
item.description: Is there anyone John Lithgow can’t — or won’t — play?
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:credit: Raphael Gaultier for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a career stretching toward 200 roles, John Lithgow has played King Lear, a serial killer, a corrupt cardinal, a dance-banning reverend, an alien and Winston Churchill (twice).

item.title: With Twin Babies, the Opera Star Lise Davidsen Wonders What Comes Next
item.description: Davidsen, who has returned triumphantly to the Met in “Tristan und Isolde,” said that singing “has been my world, my everything. And now it’s not.”
item.dc:creator: Zachary Woolfe
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times

item.title: In the Oscars Audience, Stars Caught Their Breath
item.description: Our photographer captured unguarded moments with Michael B. Jordan, Paul Thomas Anderson, Gwyneth Paltrow and other celebrities in the crowd.
item.dc:creator: Philip Cheung
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times

item.title: What to Know About Banksy and the Street Artist’s Identity
item.description: An investigation by Reuters, which says it has identified the street artist, hinges on a police report from his arrest in New York two decades ago.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu and Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Jack Taylor/Getty Images
item.media:description: Banksy transformed his artwork “Girl With Balloon” into “Love Is in the Bin” when it was shredded after being sold at auction.

item.title: ‘Ulster American’ Review: Matthew Broderick as a Star Blinded by Privilege
item.description: David Ireland’s satire follows a Hollywood actor whose cluelessness leads to a combustible confrontation.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Geraldine Hughes, Matthew Broderick and Max Baker, as a playwright, star and director in “Ulster American,” at the Irish Repertory Theater.

item.title: How Readers Voted on Miscast Roles in the Movies
item.description: Given a list of 14 mismatches, they quickly picked Ben Platt in “Dear Evan Hansen.” But they took issue (loudly) with one of our choices.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman

item.title: Grace Ives Was Tipped as a Top Indie-Rock Star. Then She Hit the Bottom.
item.description: Her 2022 breakthrough, “Janky Star,” won her accolades and devotees, but she didn’t begin a follow-up until she got sober and started fresh.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: Academy ‘Extremely Upset’ After Teyana Taylor Says Oscars Security Shoved Her
item.description: The academy said an employee of an outside security firm hired for the Oscars had “incidental contact” with the actress, which it called “not acceptable.”
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Mike Coppola/Getty Images
item.media:description: Teyana Taylor, a star of “One Battle After Another,” which won best picture at the 98th annual Academy Awards on Sunday in Los Angeles.

item.title: How Michael B. Jordan Won (and Timothée Chalamet Lost) Best Actor at 2026 Oscars
item.description: The “Sinners” star became the consensus pick as voters soured on Timothée Chalamet, the “Marty Supreme” lead. It helped that the winner is a bankable performer.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael B. Jordan with his statuette on Sunday night.

item.title: Playwrights Horizons Settles Case Over Discounts for People of Color
item.description: The lawsuit objected to a “BIPOC night” program at Playwrights Horizons, an Off Broadway nonprofit.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: House Adopts Bill to Ease Heirs’ Recovery of Art That Nazis Looted
item.description: The Senate had already passed an extension of the so-called HEAR Act, which is scheduled to expire at the end of the year. The bill now heads to President Trump’s desk.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Hickley and Zachary Small
item.category: SNCF (Societe Nationale des Chemins de fer Francais)
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The bill would extend what is known as the Holocaust Expropriated Art Recovery Act of 2016, which was designed to address cases where museums had blocked claims by invoking the statute of limitations.

item.title: Jocelyn Bioh’s ‘School Girls; Or, The African Mean Girls Play’ Heads to Broadway
item.description: “School Girls; or, The African Mean Girls Play,” written by Jocelyn Bioh and directed by Whitney White, will start performances in September.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: School Girls; or, the African Mean Girls Play (Play)
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: “School Girls; or, The African Mean Girls Play" was staged in 2017 at the Lucille Lortel Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Jonathan Groff and Daniel Radcliffe in ‘Merrily We Roll Along’ and More Theater to Stream
item.description: Joined by Daniel Radcliffe, Groff stars in the hit Broadway production of the Sondheim musical. And there are (count ’em) three productions of “The Importance of Being Earnest” this month.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Take a Banana for the Ride (Play)
What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)

item.title: For Once, the Oscars Got a Lot Right (Even the In Memoriam Segment)
item.description: The ceremony figured out how to celebrate movies and the people who make them. It even understood Robert Redford’s place in American cinema.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis and Alissa Wilkinson
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Part of the Oscar haul for “One Battle After Another,” which took six trophies.

item.title: Late Night Wonders Which Former President Spoke With Trump
item.description: In guessing which ex-leader Trump might have discussed Iran with, Jimmy Fallon said “two things seem equally possible: Either Trump’s lying, or Joe Biden doesn’t remember talking to him.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Alfredo Bryce Echenique, 87, Dies; Novelist Bared Peru’s Privileged Class
item.description: “The other Peruvian” (alongside Mario Vargas Llosa), he exposed the heedlessness of the upper crust, which he knew well, and the suffering of the underclasses.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Raphael Gaillarde/Gamma-Rapho, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Alfredo Bryce Echenique in 1999 in Paris, where he long lived in self-imposed exile. “His own social class loathed him,” his biographer said. “He would say, ‘The elitists are deeply ignorant.’”

item.title: 8 Great Songs That Make It Easy to Be Green
item.description: Celebrate St. Patrick’s Day with Ray Charles’s take on Kermit the Frog’s classic and tracks from Lorde, Booker T. & the M.G.’s, Joni Mitchell and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: CBS/Getty Images
item.media:description: Kermit the Frog and Ray Charles

item.title: Albert Zuckerman, Literary Agent and ‘Hero of the Blockbuster,’ Dies at 94
item.description: During his 50-year career, he represented dozens of best-selling authors, including Ken Follett, Stephen Hawking and Michael Lewis.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Mireya Acierto/Getty Images
item.media:description: Albert Zuckerman in 2015. He started the Writers House literary agency in 1973, working out of a 100-square-foot office above a porn store in Times Square.

item.title: Len Deighton, Author of Espionage Best-Sellers, Dies at 97
item.description: His Cold War thrillers “The Ipcress File” and “Funeral in Berlin” brought a documentary-style realism to the spy genre.
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:credit: Ulf Andersen/Getty Images
item.media:description: Len Deighton in 1986. He wrote “The Ipcress File” in reaction to the James Bond novels of Ian Fleming, which he regarded as cartoonish and morally simplistic.

item.title: Meg Webster’s Life in Five Artworks
item.description: Meg Webster revels in impermanence. Here, her story in five works.
item.dc:creator: Zoë Lescaze

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Has a Bone to Pick With Trump’s War Plans
item.description: “The only war Trump had an exit plan for was Vietnam,” Kimmel remarked after the president said he would end the war in Iran when he “feels it in his bones.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Inside the Katseye Fandom: Friendships, Rivalries and Boycotts Over Manon’s Hiatus
item.description: Fans of the group have leveraged their influence into friendship and rivalry. Then a member’s sudden hiatus sent them into a spiral.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai and Matt Yan
item.media:credit: Alexander Coggin for The New York Times
item.media:description: “You have to remind yourself that you don’t know them personally,” said Joy Lebofsky, who has experienced the joys and disappointments of being a stan.

item.title: Richard Grenell: From Trump Cabinet Hopeful to Ex-Kennedy Center Manager
item.description: Richard Grenell once hoped to be President Trump’s secretary of state. Instead, Mr. Trump just replaced him as Kennedy Center president.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Richard Grenell, left, the outgoing president of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, was seated next to his replacement, Matt Floca, the center’s vice president of facilities operations, at a White House news conference on Monday.

item.title: How ‘Marty Supreme’ and Timothée Chalamet Went Home With No Oscars
item.description: A few years ago, the indie studio A24 was luxuriating in Academy Awards. On Sunday it was shut out entirely.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: “Marty Supreme,” directed by Josh Safdie and starring Timothée Chalamet, received nine Oscar nominations and won none.

item.title: Judy Pace, 83, Dies; Actress Brought Layers to Black Characters
item.description: On the prime-time soap opera “Peyton Place,” she played one of TV’s first Black female antagonists. She was also a fixture in blaxploitation films.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: ABC Photo Archives/Disney General Entertainment Content, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Judy Pace in 1971, when she appeared in “Brian’s Song,” the critically acclaimed, hugely popular TV movie.

item.title: Live Nation Antitrust Case Resumes With Testimony About Springsteen Fees
item.description: After the Justice Department announced a deal with the concert giant, the trial picked up after a week’s break, with a coalition of states leading the way.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: In testimony on Monday, the chief executive of AEG Presents spoke about competing against Live Nation.

item.title: At Dance Reflections, Embodied Acts of Memory
item.description: Works by Soa Ratsifandrihana and Robin Orlin evoked Malagasy line dances and Zulu rickshaw drivers.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.category: Skirball Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: Oscars Cut Off ‘KPop Demon Hunters’ Speech in Brutal Live Moment
item.description: After the hit song from “KPop Demon Hunters” made Oscars history, a snare drum and a crashing cymbal mercilessly drowned out the winners.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Chris Pizzello/Invision, via Associated Press

item.title: Kennedy Center Board Votes to Close for 2-Year Renovation Project
item.description: Ahead of the vote, President Trump criticized the center’s physical condition and previous financial management.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: “What I know best in the world is construction,” President Trump said at a news conference about planned renovations at the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.

item.title: John Bengtson, Modern-Day Silent-Film Sleuth, Dies at 68
item.description: Enamored of stars like Charlie Chaplin, he matched outdoor scenes from their movies to contemporary locales, creating a visual record of vanished cityscapes.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald
item.media:credit: Michael Macor/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mr. Bengtson, in 2001, standing at the same San Francisco location where Buster Keaton (seen in the enlarged movie still he is holding) shot a silent film a century earlier.

item.title: Michael B. Jordan Wins Best Actor
item.description: Our awards season columnist Kyle Buchanan talks about the most suspenseful Oscar of 2026 — for best actor.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan, Edward Vega, Gabriel Blanco, Laura Salaberry and Léo Hamelin

item.title: Review: ‘The Silent Serenade’ at Mannes Opera
item.description: Erich Wolfgang Korngold’s “The Silent Serenade,” written in the 1940s, was given its English-language, United States premiere by Mannes Opera.
item.dc:creator: Seth Colter Walls

item.title: Review: Handel’s ‘Hercules’ at Carnegie Hall
item.description: At Carnegie Hall, the English Concert made a case for “Hercules” as a strikingly modern dramatic oratorio of psychological and musical depth.
item.dc:creator: Corinna da Fonseca-Wollheim

item.title: Where to Stream the 2026 Oscar Winners, From ‘One Battle After Another’ to ‘Sinners’
item.description: Most of the awarded films, including the winner of best picture, can be watched at home. Here’s a guide to catch up.
item.dc:creator: Scott Tobias
item.media:credit: Warner Bros. Pictures, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Chase Infiniti in “One Battle After Another.”

item.title: The Politics of Teyana Taylor’s ‘One Battle After Another’ Character
item.description: Teyana Taylor’s character of Perfidia Beverly Hills is the most radical part of Paul Thomas Anderson’s Oscar-winning movie.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: At the Governors Ball After-Party, Stars Celebrate the 2026 Oscars
item.description: Michael B. Jordan, Jessie Buckley, Paul Thomas Anderson, Leonardo DiCaprio and other nominees and winners celebrated at the official Academy Awards after-party.
item.dc:creator: Sinna Nasseri
item.media:credit: Sinna Nasseri for The New York Times

item.title: Norway Celebrates Oscar Win for Joachim Trier’s ‘Sentimental Value’
item.description: “Sentimental Value,” set in Oslo, was named best international feature film. After a slew of negative news in Norway, it was a welcome victory.
item.dc:creator: Henrik Pryser Libell and Jeffrey Gettleman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joachim Trier accepting the Oscar for his film “Sentimental Value.” He said he was “just so proud and shocked” about the movie’s success.

item.title: The Best Oscars Fashion Trend Was Dresses You Can Breathe In
item.description: It was the best trend on the Oscars red carpet.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: A different way to dress: Renate Reinsve in a strapless Louis Vuitton column.

item.title: Pope to Make a (Virtual) Visit for the United States’ 250th Birthday
item.description: After declining an invitation from President Trump, Pope Leo XIV will make a virtual appearance on July 3 at the National Constitution Center to accept its Liberty Medal.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Vincenzo Livieri/Reuters
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV, the first pontiff born in the United States, will appear via video on July 3 to accept the Liberty Medal from the National Constitution Center in Philadelphia.

item.title: Best and Worst Moments From the 2026 Oscars
item.description: There was a lot to take in, from Michael B. Jordan’s thrilling win to the perplexing “bum drum.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times

item.title: Where to Stream the Films of Paul Thomas Anderson
item.description: The director has spent three decades creating an inimitable style, as seen in films like “Boogie Nights,” “There Will Be Blood,” “Phantom Thread” and more.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey

item.title: Jess and Morgs Blur the Boundaries Between Live Performance and Film
item.description: Jess and Morgs, the creators of weird and wonderful works that blur the boundaries between live performance and digital, have reached the big time: the Paris Opera.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas
item.media:credit: Maxime La for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jessica Wright and Morgann Runacre-Temple at the Palais Garnier in Paris.

item.title: In ‘The Lady,’ a Current Royal Scandal Meets an Older One
item.description: The series, “inspired” by the story of a royal dresser later convicted of murder, is getting added attention over the former Duchess of York’s ties to Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Jillian Rayfield

item.title: Why Did Trump Officials Award $2 Million to a Small Art School in Queens?
item.description: The National Endowment for the Humanities seldom gave seven-figure grants. Now big awards flow to handpicked projects, including an institution with three full-time employees.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small, Jennifer Schuessler and Marcus Maddox
item.media:description: Jacob Collins, the founder and artistic director of Grand Central Atelier, has had a lifetime mission to inspire a return to a classical style of art.

item.title: ‘Imperfect Women,’ Plus 7 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new show starring Elisabeth Moss and Kerry Washington premieres. And Taylor Frankie Paul leads the new season of “The Bachelorette.”
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Courtesy of Apple TV.
item.media:description: Elisabeth Moss, left, and Kerry Washington in “Imperfect Women.”

item.title: Javier Bardem and Other Stars Address Wars and Free Speech at Oscars
item.description: Conan O’Brien, the ceremony’s host, noted in his opening monologue that viewers were “all too aware that these are very chaotic, frightening times.”
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jimmy Kimmel delivered one of the sharpest political jokes of Oscars night while introducing the documentary awards.

item.title: ‘One Battle After Another’ Wins Best Picture at 2026 Oscars After Awards Sweep
item.description: The epic about radicals and reactionaries also took best director for Paul Thomas Anderson, his first Academy Awards after 11 losses.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Thomas Anderson and the producer Sara Murphy accepted the Oscar for best picture as cast and crew members watched.

item.title: Jessie Buckley Caps Award Season with ‘Hamnet’ Oscar Win for Best Actress
item.description: The actress closes out a season of wins in this category with an Academy Award.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jessie Buckley accepting her best actress Oscar for “Hamnet.”

item.title: Michael B. Jordan Wins the Best Actor Oscar for ‘Sinners’
item.description: It was the actor’s first nomination for an Academy Award.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael B. Jordan, who played twins in “Sinners,” won the Oscar for best actor on Sunday night.

item.title: Paul Thomas Anderson Wins Best Director Oscar for ‘One Battle After Another’
item.description: It was his second win, after taking home the award for best adapted screenplay on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Thomas Anderson on the Oscars stage.

item.title: Autumn Durald Arkapaw Makes History as First Woman to Win Best Cinematography
item.description: The “Sinners” cinematographer was also the first woman of color to win that category. “I really want all the women in the room to stand up,” she said in her acceptance speech.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Autumn Durald Arkapaw also thanked predecessors like Ellen Kuras and Rachel Morrison.

item.title: A Surprising Oscars Tie Is the Seventh in Academy Awards History
item.description: In 1969, Katharine Hepburn and Barbra Streisand each received an Oscar in the best actress category.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Barbra Streisand stood beside the director Anthony Harvey on the Oscars stage in 1969. Streisand was accepting the best actress award, which she shared with Katharine Hepburn (Harvey was accepting on Hepburn’s behalf).

item.title: ‘The Singers’ and ‘Two People Exchanging Saliva’ Tie for Oscar
item.description: “I’m not joking,” Kumail Nanjiani said while presenting the award for best live action short film.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kumail Nanjiani announces one of the two winners for best live action short.

item.title: Barbra Streisand Pays Tribute to Robert Redford at 2026 Oscars
item.description: Streisand and Redford built a lengthy friendship after acting together in 1973’s “The Way We Were.”
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: “He was thoughtful and bold,” Barbra Streisand said of Robert Redford. “I called him an intellectual cowboy who blazed his own trail.”

item.title: Billy Crystal Leads 2026 Oscars Tribute to Rob and Michele Reiner
item.description: Billy Crystal led a tribute to the director several months after he and his wife were murdered.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Rob Reiner and his wife, Michele Singer Reiner, were killed in December.

item.title: Conan O’Brien Parodies ‘Weapons’ and Roasts Timothée Chalamet in 2026 Oscars Monologue
item.description: In his second turn as Oscars host, his monologue wasn’t as loopy as last year’s, but he displayed a range of jokes — nerdy, topical and physical.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Conan O’Brien, dressed as Gladys in “Weapons,” led children on a chase through several movie scenes.

item.title: Sean Penn Wins Best Supporting Actor for ‘One Battle After Another’ but Skips Oscars
item.description: The actor has a history with the country’s president, Volodymyr Zelensky, and had been skipping awards ceremonies.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Bernstein
item.media:credit: Arnaud Finistre/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Sean Penn at a French festival in October.

item.title: All about the new Oscar for casting.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: Amy Madigan, 75, Wins First Oscar for ‘Weapons’ as Aunt Gladys
item.description: The actress won for her role as Aunt Gladys in Zach Cregger’s horror hit.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amy Madigan accepting the supporting actress Oscar for her role in “Weapons.”

item.title: Our Favorite Oscars Red Carpet Photos Featuring Jessie Buckley and Emma Stone
item.description: Grand entrances, statement-making fashion and the occasional candid moment, on a night geared for gold.
item.dc:creator: Sinna Nasseri
item.media:credit: Sinna Nasseri for The New York Times
item.media:description: Teyana Taylor, nominated for best supporting actress in “One Battle After Another.”

item.title: Oscars 2026 Winners List: ‘One Battle After Another,’ ‘Sinners’ and More
item.description: The winning actors, directors, pictures and more at the 98th annual Academy Awards.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael B. Jordan, left, and Jessie Buckley, right, who took home the lead acting Oscars.

item.title: Matt Berry will be this year’s ‘Voice of God.’
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Jae C. Hong/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Matt Berry may be best known for “What We Do in the Shadows.”
